description: D0 is Arduino digital pin 0, which maps to ATmega328P pin PD0. It is the hardware UART receive line and also belongs to the PCINT[23:16] pin-change interrupt group.
beginnerExplanation: Arduino gives this pad the simple name D0, but the microcontroller calls the same electrical connection PD0 because it is bit 0 of port D. Serial examples may call it RX, while interrupt code may call it PCINT16.
headerPositionLabel: Top digital header, far right

description: D1 is Arduino digital pin 1, backed by PD1 on the ATmega328P. It is the hardware transmit pin for the UART and can also take part in pin-change interrupts.
beginnerExplanation: The same pad is named TX in Arduino serial tutorials, D1 in beginner sketches, and PD1 in low-level AVR code because each name describes a different layer of the same wire.
headerPositionLabel: Top digital header, second from right
exampleCode:
Serial.begin(9600);
Serial.println("TX on D1 uses the same hardware line as PD1");

description: D2 is Arduino digital pin 2, which is the same microcontroller pin as PD2. That one physical pad can be used as normal GPIO, as the dedicated external interrupt input INT0, or as pin-change interrupt source PCINT18.
beginnerExplanation: This is the classic example of one pad having many names. D2 is the board-level label, PD2 is the ATmega328P port-bit name, INT0 is its dedicated interrupt function, and PCINT18 is its grouped pin-change interrupt identity.
headerPositionLabel: Top digital header, third from right

description: D3 maps to PD3, which is both external interrupt INT1 and pin-change interrupt PCINT19. It is also tied to the Timer2 compare output OC2B, so Arduino treats it as a PWM-capable pin.
beginnerExplanation: When you call `analogWrite(3, value)`, you are really asking Timer2 hardware to wiggle the OC2B function on PD3. In an interrupt example, the very same metal pad can instead behave as INT1.
headerPositionLabel: Top digital header, fourth from right
exampleCode:
pinMode(3, OUTPUT);
analogWrite(3, 128); // Timer2 -> OC2B -> D3 / PD3

description: D4 is ordinary Arduino digital pin 4 at the board level, but in AVR naming it is PD4 and PCINT20. The ATmega328P also labels this line T0 because it can feed an external clock into Timer0.
beginnerExplanation: A board label tells you where to plug a jumper. The AVR label tells you which port register controls it. Peripheral labels like T0 tell you what special hardware can borrow the same wire.
headerPositionLabel: Top digital header, fifth from right
exampleCode:
pinMode(4, INPUT);
// PD4 can also act as T0 or PCINT20 depending on peripheral setup.

description: D5 is backed by PD5 and can emit the OC0B timer output from Timer0, which makes it a PWM-capable pin on the UNO.
beginnerExplanation: Arduino hides the timer details for you in `analogWrite`, but the PWM waveform on D5 comes from Timer0 compare hardware, not from software manually toggling the pin.
headerPositionLabel: Top digital header, sixth from right
exampleCode: analogWrite(5, 64); // Timer0 compare output OC0B on PD5

description: D6 is the Arduino label for PD6. It can be ordinary GPIO, pin-change interrupt PCINT22, Timer0 compare output OC0A for PWM, or comparator input AIN0.
beginnerExplanation: Names like OC0A and AIN0 do not mean extra pins exist. They are alternate hardware functions that all share the same PD6 connection.
headerPositionLabel: Top digital header, seventh from right
exampleCode: analogWrite(6, 200); // Timer0 compare output OC0A on D6

description: D7 is general-purpose Arduino digital pin 7, which the ATmega328P calls PD7. It is also pin-change interrupt PCINT23 and analog comparator input AIN1.
beginnerExplanation: The board label D7 is the easiest name to plug into `pinMode`, but low-level code often talks about PD7 because it is the highest bit in port D.
headerPositionLabel: Top digital header, eighth from right

description: D8 maps to PB0 and can feed the Timer1 input capture hardware as ICP1. It is also the first pin in the PORTB pin-change interrupt group.
beginnerExplanation: Timer1 can watch this pin for a precise edge and copy the current counter value into a register. That capture role is why you may see D8 called ICP1 in advanced timing examples.
headerPositionLabel: Top digital header, ninth from right
exampleCode:
pinMode(8, INPUT);
// PB0 can be used as ICP1 when Timer1 input capture is enabled.

description: D9 is PB1 on the ATmega328P, also known as OC1A when Timer1 is driving its A compare output. It is one of the main physical pins affected by Timer1 compare register setup.
beginnerExplanation: Searches for `Timer1`, `OC1A`, or `OCR1A` often lead here because the compare unit and the physical output pin are closely related. The register names configure the timer, while D9 is where you can observe the result.
headerPositionLabel: Top digital header, tenth from right
exampleCode:
pinMode(9, OUTPUT);
analogWrite(9, 128); // Timer1 compare output A -> D9 / PB1

description: D10 is Arduino digital pin 10, the SPI chip-select line SS, and AVR port pin PB2. It is also the Timer1 compare B output OC1B and pin-change interrupt PCINT2.
beginnerExplanation: SPI examples call this pin SS or CS because of its bus role. Timer examples call it OC1B because Timer1 can generate a waveform there. Register code calls it PB2 because it is bit 2 of port B.
headerPositionLabel: Top digital header, eleventh from right
exampleCode:
pinMode(10, OUTPUT);
digitalWrite(10, HIGH); // SPI slave-select idle state

description: D11 is PB3 on the ATmega328P. It is the MOSI line for SPI, pin-change interrupt PCINT3, and the OC2A PWM output from Timer2.
beginnerExplanation: If you search for MOSI you are asking about the communication role of the pin. If you search for PB3 you are asking how the AVR port registers see it. Both names lead to the same pad.
headerPositionLabel: Top digital header, twelfth from right
exampleCode:
pinMode(11, OUTPUT); // MOSI when acting as SPI controller
analogWrite(11, 180); // Timer2 compare output OC2A

description: D12 maps to PB4 and serves as the SPI MISO line. It is also a member of the PORTB pin-change interrupt group as PCINT4.
beginnerExplanation: MISO tells you the data direction in SPI, but PB4 tells you which AVR port bit to read or write when you are not using SPI hardware.
headerPositionLabel: Top digital header, thirteenth from right
exampleCode:
pinMode(12, INPUT);
// PB4 is the SPI MISO line when SPI hardware is active.

description: D13 is PB5, the SPI serial clock output SCK, and the pin associated with the onboard LED on the UNO family. It is also pin-change interrupt PCINT5.
beginnerExplanation: Beginners meet this pin as the built-in LED pin, communication examples call it SCK, and low-level code calls it PB5. Those are all labels for the same electrical node.
headerPositionLabel: Top digital header, far left
exampleCode:
pinMode(13, OUTPUT);
digitalWrite(13, HIGH); // onboard LED-related pin

description: Ground is the shared electrical reference for the board. Multiple GND pads are connected together so shields and external circuits have convenient return points.
beginnerExplanation: Ground does not map to an AVR port bit because it is not a GPIO signal. It is the electrical zero-volt reference that lets every other voltage make sense.
headerPositionLabel: Digital header ground, plus duplicated ground pads on power header
exampleCode:
// No pinMode needed.
// Connect your circuit's ground here so signal voltages share a common reference.

description: AREF is the analog reference pin used by the ADC subsystem when you select an external reference source.
beginnerExplanation: This is a board-level analog support pin rather than a normal digital I/O point. It helps the ADC decide what voltage should count as full-scale.
headerPositionLabel: Utility pins after digital header
exampleCode:
analogReference(EXTERNAL);
// Provide a safe external reference on AREF if you use this mode.

description: VIN is the board's external input voltage node. It feeds the regulator path rather than acting as a GPIO signal.
beginnerExplanation: VIN matters at the board power level, not the AVR port level. It helps power the system but is not something you configure in DDR or PORT registers.
headerPositionLabel: Bottom power header, far right
exampleCode: // VIN is for board power input, not normal sketch-level pin control.

description: A0 is analog input 0 on the Arduino board, but it is also PC0 and PCINT8 at the AVR level.
beginnerExplanation: Analog pins can still act like digital pins because they are still microcontroller pads. The analog name tells you how the Arduino API presents them, while PC0 tells you their port identity.
headerPositionLabel: Bottom analog header, far left
exampleCode: int value = analogRead(A0);

description: A1 is analog input 1 and AVR port pin PC1. It can also take part in the PORTC pin-change interrupt group.
beginnerExplanation: The Arduino analog label focuses on the ADC channel, while PC1 focuses on the GPIO bit position inside port C.
headerPositionLabel: Bottom analog header
exampleCode: int value = analogRead(A1);

description: A2 is analog input 2, AVR port bit PC2, and pin-change interrupt source PCINT10.
beginnerExplanation: Even if you only use it with `analogRead`, the microcontroller still sees the same metal connection as port C bit 2.
headerPositionLabel: Bottom analog header
exampleCode: int value = analogRead(A2);

description: A3 is analog input 3, AVR port bit PC3, and pin-change interrupt source PCINT11.
beginnerExplanation: This pin shows how the analog header is still just another group of microcontroller pads with GPIO and interrupt identity.
headerPositionLabel: Bottom analog header
exampleCode: int value = analogRead(A3);

description: A4 is analog input 4 and AVR pin PC4, but it is also the I2C data line SDA. The UNO R3 duplicates this connection onto the dedicated SDA header position listed in JDIGITAL.
beginnerExplanation: This pin is a great beginner example because it exists in both the analog header and the SDA duplicate header position. Search for A4, PC4, SDA, or PCINT12 and you still land on the same microcontroller signal.
headerPositionLabel: Bottom analog header, duplicated on top utility row
exampleCode:
#include <Wire.h>

Wire.begin(); // Uses SDA on A4 / PC4

description: A5 is analog input 5 and AVR pin PC5, and it also serves as the I2C clock line SCL. The UNO R3 duplicates this signal on the dedicated SCL header position in JDIGITAL.
beginnerExplanation: A5 and SCL are not two different wires. They are two common names for the same board connection, depending on whether you are thinking about analog input or I2C communication.
headerPositionLabel: Bottom analog header, duplicated on top utility row
exampleCode:
#include <Wire.h>

Wire.begin(); // Uses SCL on A5 / PC5
